Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (14) - Building a BMI Calculator, Part 2
We've wrapped up a decent chunk of the fundamentals at this point, so now it's time to do what we always do next — take those little knowledge points and actually build something with them.
Back at the end of day six, we wrote a simple BMI calculator:
print('Your current BMI is:', bmiNum, 'which falls in the underweight range.')
print('Your current BMI is:', bmiNum, 'which falls in the normal range.')
print('Your current BMI is:', bmiNum, 'which falls in the overweight range.')
print('Your current BMI is:', bmiNum, 'which falls in the mildly obese range.')
print('Your current BMI is:', bmiNum, 'which falls in the moderately obese range.')
print('Your current BMI is:', bmiNum, 'which falls in the severely obese range.')
print(bmi(68, 175)) # Your current BMI is: 22.2 which falls in the overweight range.
print(bmi(95, 185)) # Your current BMI is: 27.76 which falls in the moderately obese range.
Now we're going to add a few little features on top of this code.
(That image up there is honestly so handy.)
So first, let's talk through the user story.
The user can type in their height and weight through the terminal, and the order has to be height first, then weight.
Before the result shows up, a "Loading" message appears for about five seconds.
After five seconds the result prints, showing the BMI value and which range the user's BMI falls in.
For example: Your current BMI is: 22.2 which falls in the overweight range.
Let me throw in one more slightly advanced thing too.
The output text has to be colored based on the range it falls in.
For example: in the sentence "Your current BMI is: 22.2 which falls in the overweight range", the word "overweight" has to be orange.
Alright, now that you've read the user story, let's build it.
Before you scroll down, you can try building it yourself first. If you get stuck, then come back and see how I did it.
Letting the user type in height and weight

Lucky for us, we covered this exact technique in the previous chapter, so it should be fresh in your memory. We need to declare two variables for height and weight, but pay attention to one thing the user story mentioned:
"The user can type in their height and weight through the terminal, and the order has to be height first, then weight."
So we know we'll be using the

function. The next question is — everything the user types comes back as a string, so should we use a positive integer here, or a float? Usually when we measure height and weight there's a decimal point involved, so I'd recommend wrapping

cm = float(input('Please enter your height: '))
kg = float(input('Please enter your weight: '))
And that's the first user story done.
Next up is the Loading effect in the terminal. The user story said it needs to run for five seconds:
"Before the result shows up, a 'Loading' message appears for about five seconds."
The key technique here is a
, so we'll probably need to loop five times:
five times, but that's a bit ugly. So let's tweak it into
instead. To pull that off we need to use
parameter. What do I mean by that? We can see it's five dots, so we print the
text first, then loop out the dots one by one. By default
(a newline), so we need to switch it to no-newline mode:
That's already really close to the
look we want. Now we just need the dots to appear one per second. Since Python doesn't have time-related functions built in, we have to pull in Python's
function, which pauses your code for however many seconds you tell it. So we just drop this

import time

print('Loading', end='')
for i in range(5):
  print('.', end='')
  time.sleep(1) # let the code sleep for one second
time.sleep(1) # let the code sleep for one second
At this point you'll notice the output doesn't show up in real time. By the time it appears, you get a complete
all at once instead of the dots trickling out. This is because of
, the output gets buffered in memory and only printed once the whole loop finishes. Switch it to
and the result gets printed immediately instead of sitting in the buffer:

import time

print('Loading', end='')
for i in range(5):
  print('.', end='', flush=True)
  time.sleep(1) # let the code sleep for one second
is actually a pretty neat little property.
Let me tidy up the code here first. Let's merge the

import time

cm = float(input('Please enter your height: '))
kg = float(input('Please enter your weight: '))

print('Loading', end='')
for i in range(5):
  print('.', end='', flush=True)
  time.sleep(1) # let the code sleep for one second
For the BMI calculation itself, I'm going to take the lazy route and just pull in the code we already wrote earlier, with a small tweak:
At this point the code is roughly 80% done. But if you actually try running it, you'll notice the Loading text gets mixed up with the result output. So we need to adjust the
a bit — only on the very last iteration should it go back to the normal
And now the output comes out clean.
The text-color part is honestly the trickier bit. Luckily someone on Stack Overflow already worked this out:

HEADER = '\033[95m'
OKBLUE = '\033[94m'
OKCYAN = '\033[96m'
OKGREEN = '\033[92m'
WARNING = '\033[93m'
FAIL = '\033[91m'
ENDC = '\033[0m'
BOLD = '\033[1m'
UNDERLINE = '\033[4m'
So we can pull out the ones we need into our own dictionary:
Using it is dead simple. Here I'll use string formatting to recolor the text:
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["WARNING"]}underweight{color["ENDC"]} range.')
Heads up! Inside the f-string, the dictionary key has to use double quotes, otherwise you'll hit a
And with that, we've got the more advanced version of the BMI calculator done. Here's the full example for reference:
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["WARNING"]}underweight{color["ENDC"]} range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["OKGREEN"]}normal{color["ENDC"]} range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["WARNING"]}overweight{color["ENDC"]} range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["WARNING"]}mildly obese{color["ENDC"]} range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["FAIL"]}moderately obese{color["FAIL"]} range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum} which falls in the {color["FAIL"]}severely obese{color["FAIL"]} range.')

We've wrapped up a chunk of the fundamentals, so now it's time to put those little knowledge points to work. In this one we take the BMI calculator from earlier and add some real features — terminal input, a fake five-second Loading animation, and colored output text in the terminal.
